label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why the text uses a lot of figures. The options are (A) record the valuable data, (B) provide concrete evidence, (C) introduce a research method, (D) give a detailed description. First, I need to understand the role of the figures in the text. The article mentions several statistics: 30% of Chinese urban households own a credit card, 40% annual growth rate, 90% unaware of safety issues, 60% lack knowledge about debt consequences, 72% never late, 55% pay full balance, 70% say credit cards caused overspending. These numbers are presented to support the researcher's points about the implications of credit card usage in China. The main purpose of using these figures is likely to back up the claims made by Rui Yao. For example, when Yao says that there's a lack of knowledge, the 90% and 60% figures are evidence for that claim. Similarly, the 70% figure about overspending supports the idea that credit cards might lead to increased spending. So these numbers are not just recording data for their own sake, but are used to substantiate the arguments being made. Looking at the options again, (B) "provide concrete evidence" seems to fit because the figures are used to support the statements and conclusions in the text. Option (A) is about recording data, which is part of it, but the text is more focused on using that data to make
